findings.location: tech-spec.md — PaymentSplitter.sol function signature (Solution, D4, Task 4, Task 10)
findings.description: The payWithAuthorization function signature across the entire tech-spec is (developerId, value, validAfter, validBefore, nonce, v, r, s) — it does NOT include a 'from' parameter. However, USDC.transferWithAuthorization(address from, address to, uint256 value, uint256 validAfter, uint256 validBefore, bytes32 nonce, uint8 v, bytes32 r, bytes32 s) requires 'from' to be passed explicitly — the USDC contract does NOT ecrecover 'from' itself; it takes 'from' as an argument and verifies the signature was signed by that address. Task 4 description contains the comment 'from = ecrecover_inside_USDC' which is factually wrong about how USDC works. As specified, the splitter cannot call USDC.transferWithAuthorization because it has no way to supply the 'from' address. The fix is to add 'address from' as the second parameter of payWithAuthorization, e.g., payWithAuthorization(address developerId, address from, uint256 value, ...). This propagates to Task 10 settle.ts calldata construction and must also be reflected in the user-spec AC and the middleware settle call.
findings.recommendation: Add 'address from' as a parameter to payWithAuthorization in PaymentSplitter.sol, IPaymentSplitter.sol interface, Task 4 description, Task 10 settle.ts calldata, and the settle call in core.ts (step 7c in Architecture). Update user-spec AC line 162 and the Флоу step 5d accordingly. Clarify in IERC3009.sol that 'from' is an explicit input, not derived by the contract.

findings.description: The entire User-Spec Deviations section is marked '[PENDING USER APPROVAL]' for the full user-spec + tech-spec rewrite set. The status field of both documents is 'draft', so this is expected per the deviation rules. However, this means the tech-spec cannot be approved until the user explicitly approves the full rewrite. No task in the Implementation Tasks captures obtaining this approval or transitioning status from 'draft' to 'approved'. The Final Wave tasks (18, 19, 20) proceed directly to deploy without a user-approval gate.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit user-approval checkpoint between the Audit Wave and Final Wave, or mark the status fields as 'approved' once the user has signed off on the rewrite. The pre-deploy-qa task (Task 18) description should include verification that the [PENDING USER APPROVAL] deviations have been resolved before proceeding to deploy.

findings.description: User-spec AC (line 162) specifies: emit PaymentReceived(developerId, from, value, fee, block.timestamp) — 5 parameters including block.timestamp. The tech-spec defines the event as PaymentReceived(address indexed developerId, address indexed payer, uint256 value, uint256 platformFee) — 4 parameters, omitting block.timestamp. This is a functional deviation from the explicitly stated AC. Any indexer or off-chain consumer relying on the event signature to reconstruct payment history by time will be unable to do so without block.timestamp in the event.
findings.recommendation: Add 'uint256 timestamp' (or 'uint64 timestamp') as the fifth parameter to the PaymentReceived event in the Data Models section, the Task 4 contract implementation, and the Task 5 test coverage. Alternatively, document in User-Spec Deviations that block.timestamp is intentionally omitted (it is available via the block itself) and update the user-spec AC.

findings.location: tech-spec.md — Acceptance Criteria, line 388 vs user-spec line 177
findings.description: User-spec requires 100% branch coverage on PaymentSplitter.sol ('100% branch coverage на PaymentSplitter.sol'). The tech-spec Testing Strategy (line 324) also says 'target 100% branch coverage', but the Acceptance Criteria (line 388) downgrades this to ≥95%. Task 5 description matches the downgraded 95% figure. These three locations are inconsistent with each other and with the user-spec. Implementors will see 95% in the AC and stop there.
findings.recommendation: Reconcile all three locations to a single number. If 100% is the actual requirement (as in user-spec), update both the Acceptance Criteria (line 388) and Task 5 description to require 100% branch coverage. If 95% is intentional, update user-spec AC and Testing Strategy header and document it as a deviation.

findings.location: tech-spec.md — Task 5: Contract unit tests, user-spec line 177
findings.description: User-spec specifies 'Hardhat tests на локальном форке Arc Testnet' for contract tests. Task 5 in the tech-spec uses MockUsdcEip3009.sol — a mock USDC contract — instead of forking Arc Testnet. This is a deliberate deviation (mock vs. fork) that is not documented in the User-Spec Deviations section. A fork-based test would exercise the real USDC.transferWithAuthorization implementation, catching ABI incompatibilities. The mock may behave differently from the actual Circle USDC on Arc Testnet.
findings.recommendation: Document this approach change (mock vs. Arc Testnet fork) in User-Spec Deviations with a rationale (e.g., fork instability, CI speed). Alternatively, supplement mock tests with at least one fork-based test in Task 5 or Task 14. Task 3 spike is a good complement but only reads — it does not execute a payment against the real USDC.

findings.location: tech-spec.md — Architecture section / settle.ts (Task 10), Risks table
findings.description: The relayer wallet running out of USDC gas mid-settle is acknowledged in the Risks table but the mitigation is deferred to 'Pre-deploy QA verifies relayer has ≥ 1 USDC balance' and README documentation. No error handling path is specified in settle.ts or core.ts for the case where the relayer wallet has insufficient USDC to pay gas at settlement time. The transaction will fail with an out-of-gas error from the Arc network, which will surface as a generic exception in the WalletClient call. The spec does not describe how this is caught, what error code is returned to the agent (settlement_failed? a new code?), or whether the nonce store should be rolled back in this case. For a size-L feature with real money flows, this is a major gap.
findings.recommendation: Add to Task 10 (settle.ts): pre-flight balance check of relayer wallet before submitting the transaction, return a structured error (e.g., 'relayer_insufficient_funds') if balance < estimated gas cost. Add this error code to errors.ts (Task 8). Document the behavior in core.ts (Task 11): if settle returns 'relayer_insufficient_funds', return 503 Service Unavailable (not 402) since the payment itself is valid but the server cannot process it.

findings.description: The Architecture section (step 7b) and Task 9 description show the NonceStore check as two separate operations: 'check replayStore.has({from, nonce}) === false; insert'. In Node.js, if two requests with identical nonces arrive and are interleaved at async boundaries (e.g., both checked before either inserts), both could pass the has() check and both reach the settle call. While Node.js is single-threaded and synchronous code between awaits cannot be interrupted, this spec does not explicitly require that has() and insert() are called atomically within a single synchronous block without any intervening await. If an implementor adds an async step between has() and insert() (e.g., a pre-check RPC call), the TOCTOU window opens. The testing requirement 'concurrent writes safe' in the Testing Strategy does not specify what 'safe' means mechanically. For a payment system, a race allowing double-spend of the same authorization at the API level — before USDC on-chain protection kicks in — is a critical security risk.
findings.recommendation: Add to Task 9 / Task 10: explicit requirement that has() and insert() into NonceStore are called in a single synchronous block with no await between them. Define 'concurrent writes safe' in the test description as 'two simultaneous requests with the same nonce: only one proceeds to settle, the other gets nonce_already_used'. Document in replay-store.ts implementation: the check-and-insert must be a single synchronous transaction (no await between has and insert).

findings.description: No RPC timeout or transaction timeout is specified for the on-chain settle step. The Architecture section says 'await getTransactionReceipt' with no timeout boundary. On Arc Testnet, if the network stalls or the transaction is not mined within the maxTimeoutSeconds window (60 seconds per the 402 body), the agent's HTTP request will eventually time out at the HTTP layer, but the middleware may have already inserted the nonce into the replay store and submitted the transaction. The agent will get an HTTP error, not a 402, and the payment may succeed on-chain after the HTTP timeout. This creates a state where: (a) the agent's retry with the same nonce will get 'nonce_already_used' from middleware even though the API call failed, and (b) the developer will receive the payment even though no response was returned. This is a known x402 edge case that the tech-spec does not address.
findings.recommendation: Add to settle.ts: a configurable receipt timeout (default: maxTimeoutSeconds - safetyMargin, e.g., 55s). If the receipt is not received within the timeout, return settlement_failed and — critically — do NOT insert the nonce into the replay store (or remove it), so the agent can retry with the same authorization if it's still valid. Document this behavior as a known edge case in the Risks section.

findings.location: tech-spec.md — Data Models / PaymentSplitter.sol fee math
findings.description: The fee calculation 'value * feeBps / 10000' uses Solidity integer division which truncates toward zero. For small payment values (e.g., 1 micro-USDC with 50 bps fee: 1 * 50 / 10000 = 0), the platform fee rounds down to 0. The spec does not address: (1) the rounding direction (floor vs. ceiling), (2) what happens when the fee is 0 due to rounding (platformBalance not incremented, but the call still succeeds), (3) whether the sum (devShare + platformFee) always equals value. In Solidity with integer truncation, devShare = value - (value * feeBps / 10000) which is NOT the same as value * (10000 - feeBps) / 10000. The two formulas differ by at most 1 unit, but the spec uses both in different places. Task 4 must produce consistent arithmetic.
findings.recommendation: Add to Task 4: explicit fee math formula with rounding direction. Recommended: platformFee = (value * feeBps) / 10000 (truncating, meaning platform gets floor); devShare = value - platformFee (so devShare + platformFee == value exactly, no dust). Document this in the Data Models section. Add unit test in Task 5: edge case value=1 with feeBps=50 → platformFee=0, devShare=1.

findings.description: The contract Testing Strategy states 'withdraw / withdrawAll: happy path; over-withdraw reverts; reentrancy guard; respects paused/unpaused for withdraw'. The phrase 'respects paused/unpaused for withdraw' is ambiguous and contradicts D13 which states withdraw WORKS when paused. Line 330 correctly states 'withdraw works when paused'. An implementor reading line 327 first might write a test checking that withdraw is blocked when paused, which would be the wrong assertion.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite line 327 as: 'withdraw / withdrawAll: happy path; over-withdraw reverts; reentrancy guard; confirm withdraw succeeds when paused (D13 behavior)'. Remove the ambiguous 'respects paused/unpaused' phrasing.

findings.description: The architecture sequence (step 7b) checks 'validAfter <= now' but the user-spec (line 64) specifies 'validAfter: 0' as the canonical example — the common case where there is no lower-bound time restriction. The tech-spec does not specify how 'validAfter <= now' is checked when validAfter == 0 (it should always pass), nor does it specify what error code to return when validAfter > now (the authorization is not yet valid). The error catalog in Task 8 / errors.ts does not include an 'authorization_not_yet_valid' error — it only has 'authorization_expired'. If a client sends a validAfter in the future, the middleware has no specific error to return.
findings.recommendation: Add 'authorization_not_yet_valid' to the error catalog in errors.ts (Task 8). Add it to the verify.ts description in Task 9. Add a test case in Task 12: validAfter set to now+30s → 402 with authorization_not_yet_valid.

findings.location: tech-spec.md — Data Models / Developer struct, line 214
findings.description: The Developer struct includes 'uint64 registeredAt // block timestamp (informational)'. This field is not required by any user-spec AC, not used in any contract logic, and not exposed through any getter or event (DeveloperRegistered event only emits address). It adds permanent storage cost per developer registration without any functional purpose in MVP scope. No AC references this field.
findings.recommendation: Remove registeredAt from the Developer struct for MVP. If developer registration time is needed for analytics, it can be read from the DeveloperRegistered event log off-chain. If retained, document it as a [TECHNICAL] addition in User-Spec Deviations.

findings.location: tech-spec.md — Data Models / NetworkConfig type, line 246
findings.description: NetworkConfig includes a 'caip2: string' field (e.g., 'eip155:5042002') which is not referenced in any user-spec AC, not used in the x402 wire format as specified (the x402 spec uses 'network' as a string identifier like 'arc-testnet'), and not consumed by any other module in the architecture. It adds a field to the NETWORKS registry that has no defined consumer.
findings.recommendation: Remove caip2 from NetworkConfig for MVP. If needed for future x402 network identifier canonicalization, add as a post-MVP item with a user-spec AC that requires it.

findings.description: The Architecture defines IPaymentSplitter.sol as a Solidity interface for a contract that has exactly one implementation (PaymentSplitter.sol) and is only ever called from the middleware via viem/ABI — not via Solidity inheritance or polymorphism. In Solidity, defining a separate interface for a single concrete implementation adds file count and maintenance overhead without enabling any testability or swappability benefit that a mock couldn't provide directly. The middleware does not import IPaymentSplitter.sol; it uses an ABI JSON. IERC3009.sol is justified (it defines the external USDC contract shape used in the call). IPaymentSplitter.sol is not.
findings.recommendation: Remove IPaymentSplitter.sol from the Architecture and Task 4 scope. Keep IERC3009.sol as it defines an external dependency. The middleware settle.ts can inline the ABI fragment for payWithAuthorization directly (3 lines). This is YAGNI — no current consumer needs the interface.

findings.location: tech-spec.md — D5: Open registration / User-Spec Deviations
findings.description: D5 is marked [TECHNICAL] and states open registration is justified because 'A malicious actor pre-registering someone else's wallet only enables payments to that wallet, not theft.' This is a non-obvious security property that deserves user awareness. The User-Spec Deviations section mentions it was already updated in the user-spec, but the user-spec AC (line 161) does state 'Регистрация открытая (anyone может вызвать register(anyAddress))' confirming it's in scope. This is correctly documented as a technical decision. No action needed on coverage — flagged for awareness.
findings.recommendation: No change required. D5 is adequately documented and the user-spec AC reflects it.

findings.description: The error error catalog in Task 8 includes 'malformed_payment_header', and the Architecture (step 7a) says 'decodeXPayment(header) → PaymentPayload (or 400 if malformed)'. However, the Architecture says the response for malformed headers is HTTP 400, while all other errors return HTTP 402. The user-spec does not specify a 400 response — it only specifies 402 responses for payment errors. The error catalog builder in Task 8 returns status 402 for all errors including malformed_payment_header. There is a contradiction between the Architecture returning 400 for malformed and the errors.ts returning 402. Implementors will make inconsistent choices.
findings.recommendation: Decide and document: should malformed X-PAYMENT headers return 402 (consistent with x402 spec, which says all payment-related errors use 402) or 400 (technically correct for client errors)? The x402 v1 spec returns 402 with an error field even for malformed headers. Update either the Architecture sequence (step 7a) to say 402 or update errors.ts to return 400 for malformed_payment_header specifically. Remove the ambiguity.

summary: 31/38 requirements covered. 3 critical findings, 5 major, 7 minor. The most severe issue is a functional correctness bug: payWithAuthorization is missing the 'from' parameter required by USDC.transferWithAuthorization — the contract as specified cannot be implemented. Two other critical findings: a missing block.timestamp in the PaymentReceived event signature (deviation from user-spec AC) and pending user approval blocking deploy. Major underengineering gaps include missing relayer gas exhaustion error handling, unspecified settlement timeout behavior, TOCTOU risk on NonceStore check-insert atomicity, and unspecified fee math rounding. Minor issues include registeredAt scope creep, caip2 YAGNI field, IPaymentSplitter.sol YAGNI interface, branch coverage inconsistency (100% vs 95%), mock-vs-fork deviation not documented, and a 400/402 ambiguity for malformed headers.
